name: Phi Phraya
description: The Phi Phraya is a powerful and fearsome spirit in Burmese mythology, specifically associated with royalty and governance. It is not necessarily malevolent, but wields significant power and demands respect. Phi Phraya are believed to be the spirits of deceased kings or high-ranking officials who, due to their merit or unfinished business, continue to exert influence on the earthly realm. They are often associated with the protection of cities, kingdoms, and royal families. Their power can manifest in various ways, from influencing political events to causing natural disasters if angered or disrespected. Some believe that offerings and rituals must be performed regularly to appease the Phi Phraya and ensure prosperity and stability. Failure to do so can result in misfortune and chaos. They are often depicted as having a regal appearance, sometimes with golden skin and elaborate royal attire, reflecting their former status as rulers. Their influence is particularly strong in matters of state and the well-being of the kingdom, making them both revered and feared figures in Burmese folklore. They are considered to be guardians of justice and upholders of the established order, punishing those who violate the laws and traditions of the land. Therefore, understanding and respecting the Phi Phraya is crucial for maintaining harmony and prosperity within the community.
subtitle: Royal spirit of power and governance
appearance: Often depicted with golden skin, wearing elaborate royal attire and jewelry. May appear as a human or a shimmering, ethereal figure radiating power and authority. Sometimes accompanied by symbolic animals such as elephants or peacocks.
habitat: Royal palaces, ancient cities, sacred groves, and areas of significant historical or political importance. They are also believed to reside in spirit houses or shrines dedicated to their worship.
behavior: Generally benevolent if respected and appeased, but can be wrathful and vengeful if angered or neglected. They demand respect and adherence to tradition. They are often invoked for guidance and protection in matters of state and personal well-being.

keyMyths.title: The Founding of the City
keyMyths.description: A Phi Phraya guided the founders of a great city, revealing the ideal location and providing protection against enemies. Offerings were made to the spirit to ensure the city's continued prosperity and stability.
keyMyths.source: Oral tradition passed down through generations

keyMyths.title: The Wrath of the Phi Phraya
keyMyths.description: When a king neglected his duties and disrespected the traditions of the land, the Phi Phraya unleashed a series of disasters upon the kingdom. The people suffered until the king repented and restored the proper rituals.

sources.author: Htin Aung
sources.description: A collection of Burmese folk tales, including stories about spirits and deities.
sources.text: A collection of Burmese folk tales, including stories about spirits and deities.

sources.author: Maung Htin
sources.work: Customary Laws of the Burmese Buddhist Peoples
sources.description: Details on customary laws and beliefs, often intertwined with spirit worship.
sources.text: Details on customary laws and beliefs, often intertwined with spirit worship.
